metadata.name: Daily Reflection Tree
metadata.description: A 48-hour end-of-day reflection tool exploring agency, contribution, and transcendence
metadata.author: DT Fellowship Assignment

nodes.text:
Good evening. Before you go home, let's take 10 minutes to look at your day.

No right answers here. Just honest reflection.
How would you describe today in one word?

options.label: Productive
Mixed
Tough
Frustrating

metadata.purpose: Establish baseline frame - how the person is narrating today

nodes.text: You said it was {A1_OPEN.label}. When something went well today, what made the difference?

options.label: I prepared well or had clear thinking
The team showed up or I got the right support
I got lucky or things broke my way
I adapted on the fly or found a workaround

metadata.purpose: For those framing today positively, surface: does agency feel owned or attributed to luck?

nodes.text: You said it was {A1_OPEN.label}. When something didn't go as planned, what did you do?

options.label: I looked for what I could control or adjust
I talked to someone who could help
I pushed through and kept doing my best
I felt stuck and waited for the situation to change

metadata.purpose: For those framing today as difficult, surface: did they find agency even when stuck?

nodes.text: Here's the harder one: Did you tell yourself a story today about why things happened the way they did? What was the story?

options.label: I made it happen — I'm responsible
We made it happen together
It just happened — luck or timing
Others caused it or circumstances beyond me

metadata.purpose: Narrative reveals deep belief. All paths converge here to deepen before bridge.

nodes.text: You see your hand in what happened today. You prepared, or you thought on your feet. That's agency — not everything went your way, but you stayed in the driver's seat.
You turned something well because you brought people with you. That's a different kind of agency — not solo, but cooperative. Not luck, but relationship.
You're attributing today's wins to luck. Maybe. Or maybe you're underselling yourself. Good preparation feels invisible — we just call it luck. Keep that in mind.
Tough day, and you found your agency anyway. You asked for help or adjusted course. That's not winning — that's resilience. That counts.

You kept going. You pushed through. That's effort, and it matters. But effort alone can hide a question: somewhere in that push, did you have a choice about how to respond? Often we do, even when it doesn't feel like it.

Tough day, and it felt like the world was happening *to* you, not with you. Stuck. Fair enough. But even in being stuck, you're still here, still present. Let's look at that narrative you told yourself.

metadata.tone: validating difficulty, opening door to choice

nodes.text: So today's story, as you tell it, is one where you (or you and others together) made things happen. That's the internal locus — not 'everything went well' but 'I was in it.' That orientation shapes what comes next.

So the story is that things happened, circumstances drove the day, others played the lead role. That's the external locus. And there's always some truth to it — we're not islands. But it's worth asking: in what moment today did *you* still have a choice, even a small one?
Okay. You've looked at agency — at how much of today you see yourself in. Now let's shift. Not 'how did I handle things' but 'what did I give'. Because these are connected.
Think about what you gave today—and what you needed. Which resonates more?

options.label: I helped someone with something outside my role
I taught or mentored someone
I felt frustrated that I had to do others' work
I needed support that I didn't feel I got

metadata.purpose: Direct question about giving vs. receiving/entitlement

nodes.text: Good. When you {A2_OPEN.label}, what made you take that action?

options.label: I saw someone needed help
It was the right thing to do
I've earned the right to contribute / deserve to matter
I was worried how things would look if I didn't

nodes.text: {A2_OPEN.label}. When you look at that moment, what did you feel?

options.label: Responsible — it needed doing
Resentful — like I was doing someone else's job
Invisible — nobody noticed or appreciated it
Depleted — I gave and got nothing back

metadata.purpose: Distinguishes between healthy boundary-setting (responsible) and entitlement (resentful, invisible)

nodes.text: You saw it clearly: someone needed, so you helped. Or it was right, so you did it. That's contribution without keeping score. It's the rarest kind, because it asks nothing back.
You contributed because you wanted it to count — to show your worth. That's contribution still, but with a ledger. The giving is real, but you're hoping someone notices.

You acted out of worry about image — how you'd look if you didn't. That's a limit on true contribution. It's motivated by avoiding bad (shame) instead of moving toward good (helping). See the difference?

metadata.tone: gently pointing out the subtle distinction

nodes.text: You did extra work, and you felt responsible for it. That's healthy — you own what you do. But you didn't resent it. That boundary is clear.

Resentment. Invisibility. Depletion. These are the tells of entitlement — not that you expected *too much*, but that you expected *recognition* for effort. The problem isn't the work. It's the unspoken deal you thought existed.
One more on this: If you did something well today, did you need someone to notice?

options.label: Yes, it would feel good if they noticed
No — I knew it was right, that was enough
Yes, and I was frustrated they didn't
I expected to be recognized for my effort

metadata.purpose: Final probe on contribution vs. entitlement lens

nodes.text: You're oriented toward contribution. You give, and you're not keeping an invisible ledger that says 'the world owes me.' That's healthy. That's what builds belonging.
You're waiting for recognition. You gave, you did the work, and somewhere you expect a return. That expectation is running in the background, coloring how you see the day. It's worth noticing.
So you've looked at agency and contribution. One more shift: not 'how I showed up' but 'who I showed up for.' This is the widest lens.
When you think about today's biggest moment or challenge—who comes to mind?

options.label: Just me — it was my responsibility to solve
My team — we were all navigating it
A specific person who was struggling more
The customer or end-user we serve

metadata.purpose: Maps radius of concern: self → team → other → systemic

nodes.text: When something went wrong today, did you wonder what it was like for someone else involved?

options.label: I was focused on how it affected me and my work
I wondered how my team was affected
I explicitly asked someone how they were
I thought about the impact on the customer or end-user

metadata.purpose: Measures actual perspective-taking, not just awareness

nodes.text: What gave you the most meaningful moment in today?

options.label: An achievement or win for me or my work
A moment I solved something with the team
I helped someone get through something hard
I realized I was part of something bigger than myself

nodes.text: You're oriented toward your own achievement. That's not selfish—it's a frame. But the research says meaning doesn't really come from winning for yourself. It comes from knowing you mattered to someone else.

You found meaning in solving something together. That's the beginning of transcendence—you're not alone in the work. You see the team. From here, the next step is seeing beyond the team to who you're serving.

You felt it today: meaning came from being useful to someone else, or recognizing your place in something larger. That's self-transcendence. Maslow called it the peak of human development. That's the source you should pay attention to.

Today you showed us something.

**Agency:** You saw yourself as {axis1.dominant_narrative}.

**Contribution:** You oriented toward {axis2.dominant_narrative}.

**Concern:** Your lens was {axis3.dominant_narrative}.

These three together—agency, contribution, and transcendence—that's what shows up as presence. As being grown. As maturity.

One question before you go: what shifted for you in this conversation?
What, if anything, surprised you about your own answers?

options.label: I saw more agency than I gave myself credit for
I realized I give more to others than I think
I think about others more than I expected
Nothing surprising—my answers felt aligned with who I am

metadata.purpose: Meta-reflection: does the person recognize the pattern emerging?

nodes.text: Thank you. That's it for today. Come back tomorrow. You're building something worth building.
